docs.title: 🌲 Farming Simulator 25 Forestry Guide
docs.text: Forestry is one of the most overlooked money-making opportunities in Farming Simulator 25. While most players focus on crops and animals, a well-managed forestry operation can generate substantial passive income with surprisingly little ongoing effort. Whether you're clearing trees to expand your fields or running a dedicated logging business, this guide covers everything you need to know.

docs.title: 🌳 Why Do Forestry in FS25?
docs.text:
Forestry offers several unique advantages over traditional crop farming:

Advantage Why It Matters Low ongoing cost Once you own the equipment, there's no seed, fertilizer, or lime cost Year-round work Unlike crops, logging works in any season — even winter Passive income Plant a forest once and harvest it years later with minimal maintenance Land clearing bonus Clearing trees from your own fields gives you more arable land AND timber to sell High-value products Processed wood (planks, furniture) rivals the most profitable crops

Tip: Forestry pairs perfectly with a crop farm. While your crops grow, you can log. While fields are snow-covered in winter, you can process wood. It fills the downtime.

docs.title: 🌲 Tree Types
docs.text:
Farming Simulator 25 features several tree species, each with different growth times, wood values, and best uses.

Tree Growth Time Wood Value per Load Best Use Harvest Difficulty Pine 2–3 years (in-game) Medium Planks, construction Easy — straight trunk Oak 4–5 years High Furniture, high-value planks Medium — thick trunk, heavy Birch 2–3 years Medium Planks, wood chips Easy — slender trunk Poplar 12 months Low per tree Wood chips (biomass) Very Easy — chips whole Spruce 3–4 years Medium-High Construction timber Medium — branches complicate cutting

docs.title: Poplar — The Special Case
docs.text:
Poplar deserves special attention because it behaves differently from other trees. You plant poplar with a specialized planter (not a tree planter), and it's harvested with a forage harvester fitted with a poplar header — not with chainsaws or logging equipment. Poplar is grown specifically for wood chips used in biomass heating plants.

Feature Poplar Regular Trees Planted with Poplar planter (planter section) Tree planter (forestry section) Harvested with Forage harvester + poplar header Chainsaw / harvester head Output Wood chips only Logs, planks, or wood chips Best for Biogas plant, heating plant Sawmill, furniture factory

docs.title: 🪓 Equipment Needed
docs.text: Forestry requires specialized equipment. Unlike crop farming where a tractor can do many jobs, logging really demands purpose-built tools.

docs.title: Core Equipment
docs.text: Equipment Cost Use Pros Cons Chainsaw $250 Hand-cutting trees Cheap, simple, no vehicle needed Slow, can't handle large timber alone Tree Harvester $180K–$280K Fells, de-limb, cuts to length Fast, precise, automated Expensive, needs skill to operate Forwarder $140K–$200K Hauls logs from forest to roadside Carries multiple logs, gentle on terrain Doesn't cut trees, needs a harvester partner Skidder $80K–$120K Drags whole trees to landing Cheaper than forwarder, simpler operation Damages ground, less capacity Wood Chipper $40K–$120K Chips whole trees into wood chips Direct-to-trailer chipping, good for biomass Creates low-value product per tree Logging Trailer $20K–$50K Hauls cut logs on tractor Budget-friendly, uses existing tractor Manual loading, lower capacity Crane / Loader $30K+ (as attachment) Loads logs onto trailers Essential for manual logging Tricky to master

docs.title: Budget vs Pro Setup
docs.text:
Setup Total Cost What You Get Best For Budget Logger ~$1,000 Chainsaw + basic logging trailer Small-scale clearing, contract work Mid-Range Logger ~$120K Chainsaw + tractor + logging trailer + crane Medium forestry operation Professional ~$400K Tree harvester + forwarder + logging truck Full-time logging business Wood Chip Farmer ~$200K Forage harvester + poplar header + trailer Dedicated poplar chip operation

Tip: Start with just a chainsaw and a logging trailer. Do a few forestry contracts to earn enough for a proper harvester and forwarder. Don't buy the expensive equipment until you know you enjoy forestry.

docs.title: 🏭 Wood Processing
docs.text: Raw logs are worth money, but processing them multiplies their value significantly.

docs.title: Sawmill — Logs to Planks
docs.text:
Input Output Value Added Time 1,000 L Pine Logs 600 L Planks ~+80% over raw logs Fast 1,000 L Oak Logs 500 L Planks (higher quality) ~+100% over raw logs Fast 1,000 L Mixed Logs 550 L Planks (variable) ~+90% over raw logs Fast

Planks can be: Sold directly, used in production chains (furniture), or stored for high-price selling windows.

docs.title: Wood Chips — Logs to Biomass
docs.text: Input Output Value Added Best Buyer 1,000 L Logs ~800 L Wood Chips +40% over selling raw logs Heating plant 1,000 L Poplar ~900 L Wood Chips +60% over selling raw poplar Biogas plant

docs.title: Furniture Factory — Planks to Furniture
docs.text:
Input Output Value Added Notes 100 L Planks + other materials 1 Unit Furniture ~+200% Requires production chain building

Tip: If you don't own a sawmill, sell raw logs to the lumber yard. If you do own one, always process logs into planks first. The profit uplift is substantial and processing is fast.

docs.title: 📋 Forestry Contracts
docs.text:
Forestry contracts appear on the map as blue icons with a tree symbol. They're a great way to earn money and learn the logging mechanics.

Contract Type Typical Payout Time Equipment Provided Difficulty Clear trees (small area) $5K–$10K 15–30 min Chainsaw + trailer Easy Clear trees (medium area) $10K–$20K 30–60 min Harvester + forwarder Medium Clear trees (large area) $20K–$50K 60–120 min Full forestry fleet Hard Harvest specific tree type $8K–$15K 20–40 min Harvester + forwarder Medium

docs.title: Forestry Contract Tips
docs.text:
1. Use the borrowed equipment — Contracts provide free use of forestry equipment. This is how you try expensive machines before buying them.
  2. Keep the wood — Some contracts let you keep the harvested timber as bonus income on top of the contract payment.
  3. Check the terrain — Steep, rocky slopes make logging significantly harder. Avoid these contracts as a beginner.
  4. Bundle contracts — Accept multiple forestry contracts in the same area. Clear them simultaneously for efficiency.
  5. Watch the deadline — Forestry contracts take longer than field work. Don't accept one if you can't finish it.

Tip: Forestry contracts are among the highest-paying early-game activities, but they require more manual skill than field contracts. Practice with a chainsaw on your own land first.

docs.title: 📈 Best Forestry Strategy by Game Stage
Early Game (First $100K)
docs.text:
Goal: Earn capital while learning forestry basics.

  1. Buy a chainsaw ($250) — the cheapest tool in the game
  2. Cut down trees on your own property (most starting farms have some)
  3. Load logs manually onto a trailer and sell to the lumber yard
  4. Do forestry contracts for larger payouts using borrowed equipment
  5. Save up for a tree harvester

Expected income: $2K–$5K per hour of manual logging

docs.title: Mid Game ($100K–$500K)
docs.text:
Goal: Build a dedicated forestry operation.

  1. Buy a tree harvester (John Deere 909MH or similar)
  2. Buy a forwarder to move logs efficiently
  3. Work through contracts and your own forest simultaneously
  4. Consider buying a forested field — the timber alone can pay for the land
  5. Start planting your own forest in a dedicated area

Expected income: $15K–$30K per hour with harvester + forwarder

docs.title: Late Game ($500K+)
docs.text:
Goal: Vertical integration — own the full wood processing chain.

  1. Buy or build a sawmill production building
  2. Process all logs into planks for 80–100% value uplift
  3. Add a furniture factory for maximum processing profit
  4. Run a poplar plantation for year-round wood chip income
  5. Use wood chips to power a biogas plant for passive electricity income

Expected income: $50K–$100K+ per harvest season with full processing chain

docs.title: 🌱 Planting Your Own Forest
docs.text: Unlike crops that must be replanted every season, trees are a multi-year investment that requires almost no maintenance.

docs.title: How to Plant a Forest
docs.text:
1. Choose a field or plot of land — It doesn't need to be arable; hilly or rocky land works fine for forestry
  2. Cultivate the ground — Just like preparing for crops
  3. Use a tree planter — Attach it to a tractor and plant in neat rows
  4. Wait — Trees grow automatically over in-game years
  5. Harvest — Use a tree harvester for maximum efficiency

docs.title: Spacing Guide
docs.text: Tree Type Recommended Spacing Trees per Hectare Pine 3m x 3m ~1,111 Oak 5m x 5m ~400 Birch 3m x 3m ~1,111 Poplar 1.5m x 0.9m ~7,400

docs.title: Recommended Forest Layout
docs.text:
Plant trees in a grid pattern with access lanes every 10 rows. This lets you drive a harvester and forwarder between the rows when it's time to harvest. A 2-hectare forest can yield $50K–$100K worth of timber per harvest cycle.

Tip: Plant pine — it grows fastest and delivers the best balance of speed and value. Dedicate 1–2 fields to pine trees, harvest every 2–3 in-game years, and enjoy a reliable cyclical income that never needs fertilizer or weeding.

docs.title: 💡 Advanced Forestry Tips
docs.text:
1. Chainsaw technique — Cut a notch on the side you want the tree to fall, then cut from the opposite side. This lets you direct trees away from buildings or equipment.
  2. Deal with branches — Most harvesters automatically de-limb trees. If using a chainsaw, cut branches off before cutting the trunk into logs.
  3. Log length matters — Cut logs to 6–8 meter lengths for easiest handling. Longer logs are harder to load but worth slightly more per cubic meter.
  4. Use the log fork — A front-loader with a log fork attachment makes manual log loading much easier than trying to grab logs with a bucket.
  5. Stack neatly — Pile logs parallel to each other at the landing area. A tidy stack is easier to load onto trucks.
  6. Night logging — Install lights on your forestry equipment. You can log at night just as efficiently as during the day.
  7. Combine with land clearing — When buying a new field that's partly forested, the timber you harvest from it can offset the purchase price by 20–40%.

Forestry isn't for everyone — it requires patience, precision, and a willingness to learn the crane controls. But for those who master it, the forest offers a reliable income stream that keeps paying year after year, completely independent of crop prices, fertilizer costs, or seasonal cycles. For more on maximizing your income, see the Profit Guide.

docs.text:
🎮 Steam 🛒

Disclosure: Affiliate links. We may earn a small commission at no extra cost to you.

docs.title: Choosing a Map
docs.text:
Map Features Recommendation Riverbend Springs Beginner-friendly, orderly fields ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Hutan Pantai Southeast Asian style, small terraced fields ⭐⭐⭐ Zielonka European style, medium farms ⭐⭐⭐⭐

Recommended for beginners: Riverbend Springs — orderly fields, flat roads, and shops at a reasonable distance.

docs.title: Game Modes
docs.text: Mode Description Recommendation New Farmer Comes with basic equipment and fields, ready to go Best for beginners Farm Manager Plenty of funds, large fields, suitable for experienced players ❌ Don't pick as a beginner Start from Scratch Only empty land + small funds, buy everything yourself ❌ Try after some experience

docs.title: 1️⃣ Get Familiar with Controls
docs.text: Action Key (PC) Get in/out of vehicle F Start engine E Accelerate/Brake W/S Open menu Esc Map M Hire worker H Switch vehicle via Tab Tab

docs.title: 2️⃣ Check Your Assets
docs.text: Press M to open the map and check: - Where your fields are (green highlight) - Where your farm is (house icon) - Where the shop is (tool icon) - Where selling points are (different colored icons)

docs.title: 3️⃣ First Income
docs.text:
Fastest way to make money:

  1. Drive the tractor to your field
  2. Press V to switch tools → select plow or seeder
  3. Press H to hire a worker to help
  4. Drive another vehicle to haul goods for extra cash

docs.title: Crop Selection
docs.text:
Crop Growth Time Profit Good for Beginners 🌾 Wheat Fast Medium ✅ Best choice 🌽 Corn Medium Medium-High ✅ Steady 🌻 Sunflowers Fast Medium ✅ Plantable Barley Fast Low ❌ Low profit Canola Medium Medium ✅ Decent

Suggested first crop: Wheat — grows fast, steady profit, and can feed cows.

docs.title: Planting Process
docs.text:
Plow → Fertilize → Seed → Wait for Growth → Harvest → Sell
 |        |        |          |           |
Optional  Recommended  Required  Watch weather  Watch prices

docs.title: When to Upgrade Equipment?
docs.text: Signal Time to upgrade Always waiting for workers to finish ✅ Buy a second tractor Harvesting too slow ✅ Upgrade harvester No space to store grain ✅ Expand silo Earned $200,000+ ✅ Buy new land

docs.title: Recommended Purchase Order
docs.text:
1. Second tractor + trailer → haul goods for profit
2. Larger seeder → improve efficiency
3. Own harvester → no more renting
4. New land → expand scale
5. Animal pen → raise cows/sheep/chickens

docs.title: Raising Chickens Basics
docs.text: Chickens are the most cost-effective starting livestock: - ❌ Low cost (coop ~$5,000) - ✅ Collect eggs daily, guaranteed profit - ✅ Don't need much feed (free-range works) - ✅ Eggs auto-deliver to the coop, hassle-free

docs.title: ❓ Why did my worker stop moving?
docs.text: Possible issues: tree in the way, out of fuel, tool not lowered. Press H to re-hire.

docs.title: ❓ Not enough money?
docs.text:
• Take out a loan (Press P → Menu → Finances)
  • Do contract missions (blue icons on the map)
  • Start with cheap crops, don't buy the most expensive equipment right away

docs.title: ❓ How to sell grain?
docs.text:
1. Harvested grain is automatically stored in the silo
  2. Drive a trailer to the silo and press R to load
  3. Check which selling point has the best price on the map
  4. Drive there and press R to unload → get paid

docs.title: Efficiency Tips
docs.text:
Tip Description 🏃 Tab to Switch No need to get out, just Tab to switch to nearby vehicles 🗺️ Use Map to Check Prices Real-time prices at each selling point, sell to the highest 🤝 Do Contracts Earn money helping others, and practice with your equipment 💰 Price Fluctuations Wait for high prices to sell, don't rush to sell low 🚜 Rent vs Buy Buy frequently used equipment, rent occasional use

Core strategy: First plant wheat to save your seed money → buy a second vehicle for hauling → expand fields → raise chickens → buy a harvester → scale up planting. By your 3rd hour, you should have a steady cash flow.

docs.title: ⛽ Fuel Consumption Comparison
docs.text:
Real fuel costs matter for large operations. Here's how popular tractors stack up.

Model HP Fuel Use (L/hr avg) Tank Size (L) Runtime per Tank Annual Fuel Cost* Fendt 500 Vario 160 HP 12 L/hr 120 L ~10 hr $1,440 Massey Ferguson 5S.145 145 HP 10 L/hr 95 L ~9.5 hr $1,200 John Deere 6R 150 150 HP 14 L/hr 110 L ~7.8 hr $1,680 Fendt 700 Vario 220 HP 16 L/hr 180 L ~11.2 hr $1,920 Case IH Magnum 250 250 HP 20 L/hr 200 L ~10 hr $2,400 Fendt 1000 Vario 400 HP 28 L/hr 400 L ~14.2 hr $3,360 Case IH Steiger 370 370 HP 30 L/hr 350 L ~11.6 hr $3,600

*Based on 120 operating hours/year at ~$1/L. Fendt tractors consistently have the best fuel economy in their class.

docs.title: 📋 Lease vs Buy — Cost Analysis

docs.title: 🗺️ Farming Simulator 25 Maps & Field Guide
docs.text: One of the first and most important decisions in Farming Simulator 25 is choosing your map. Your choice determines the shape of your fields, the distances you'll drive, the terrain you'll work, and ultimately how profitable your farm can be. This guide covers every starting map, the complete field management cycle, land purchase strategy, and how to set up your farm for maximum efficiency.

docs.title: 🏞️ Map Overview
docs.text:
Farming Simulator 25 ships with three starting maps, each with a distinct character and playstyle. A fourth map is available via the base game's optional download, and many more through DLC and mods.

Map Size Difficulty Terrain Best For Starting Fields Riverbend Springs Large Easy Flat, open fields, gentle slopes Beginners, large-scale farming ~15 fields (small to large) Zielonka Medium Medium Rolling hills, European patchwork Intermediate players, mixed farming ~20 fields (varied sizes) Hutan Pantai Medium Hard Southeast Asian style, terraced fields, narrow roads Experienced players, rice farming ~12 fields (small, irregular) Haut-Beyleron DLC Medium Medium French countryside, vineyards Wine and grape production ~10 fields + vineyards

Tip: If this is your first time playing a Farming Simulator game, start with Riverbend Springs. The flat terrain makes it easy to learn equipment handling, AI worker paths are cleaner, and the fields are logically arranged.

docs.title: Riverbend Springs — The Beginner's Choice
docs.text:
Riverbend Springs is the default starting map and the best choice for new players. The fields are large, rectangular, and well-organized. Roads are paved and straight, making it easy to navigate with wide equipment. The shop, selling points, and animal dealer are within reasonable distance of the starting farm.

Pros: - Flat terrain — no awkward slopes that cause equipment to tip - Large rectangular fields — AI workers rarely get stuck - All services within a short drive - Good mix of field sizes for early and mid-game expansion

Cons: - Can feel a bit plain compared to the other maps - Some may find it too easy

docs.title: Zielonka — The European Classic
docs.text:
Zielonka is inspired by Central European farming landscapes. Fields are smaller and more irregular than Riverbend Springs, separated by hedgerows and narrow lanes. This map rewards careful planning and is excellent for players who want a more authentic European farming experience.

Pros: - Beautiful scenery with varied terrain - More interesting field layouts - Plenty of production chain opportunities

Cons: - AI workers struggle with irregular field shapes - Narrow roads can be tricky with large equipment - Requires more manual driving

docs.title: Hutan Pantai — For Experienced Farmers
docs.text:
Set in a Southeast Asian-inspired landscape, Hutan Pantai features terraced fields, tight roads, and a distinct rice-farming economy. This map introduces unique challenges like water management for paddy fields and very small, irregular plots.

Pros: - Unique rice farming mechanics - Beautiful, detailed environment - Rewards precision driving and planning

Cons: - Not suitable for large American-style equipment - AI workers frequently get confused - Tight spaces make every job slower

Tip: Don't start on Hutan Pantai as your first FS25 experience. Try it after you've learned the basics on Riverbend Springs.

docs.title: 🌾 Field Management: The Complete Cycle
docs.text: Understanding the full field management cycle is essential to maximizing your crop yields and profits. Each step in the cycle affects your final harvest yield percentage.

docs.title: The 8-Step Cycle
docs.text: Step Equipment Needed Effect on Yield When Cost 1. Plowing Plow or subsoiler Required for root crops; +15% yield if field was plowed previously After harvest (every 3 harvests) Low 2. Cultivating Cultivator, disc harrow, or power harrow Prepares seedbed; required before seeding After plowing or direct from stubble Low 3. Liming Lime spreader +15% yield bonus Every 3 harvests (when pH drops) Medium 4. Seeding Seeder, planter, or direct drill Starts the crop After cultivating Varies by crop 5. Fertilizing Fertilizer spreader, sprayer, or slurry tank +12.5% per stage (max 2 stages = +25%) At seeding and during growth Medium 6. Rolling Roller +2.5% yield (also pushes stones down) After seeding Low 7. Weeding Weeder or herbicide sprayer Prevents up to -15% yield loss During growth stages Low-Medium 8. Harvesting Combine harvester + header Collects the crop At maturity Equipment cost

docs.title: Maximum Yield Strategy
docs.text:
To achieve 100% yield on any field:

  1. Plow the field (if needed — look for the "needs plowing" state in the menu)
  2. Lime if the soil pH is low (check soil composition map)
  3. Cultivate to prepare the seedbed
  4. Fertilize (Stage 1) — apply just before or at seeding
  5. Seed the crop
  6. Roll after seeding for a small bonus
  7. Weed mechanically or chemically as weeds appear
  8. Fertilize (Stage 2) — apply during the growth phase

Tip: Using precision farming DLC allows you to see exact soil nutrient levels and apply fertilizer only where needed, saving 20–30% on fertilizer costs while still achieving maximum yield.

docs.title: 🏡 Land Purchase Guide
docs.text: Buying land is the single most impactful investment you can make in FS25. More land means more crops, more income, and more scaling potential. But not all land is equal.

docs.title: When to Buy Land
docs.text: Phase Net Worth Strategy Early Game Under $200K Don't buy yet. Focus on equipment and doing contracts. Early-Mid $200K–$500K Buy your first additional field — small to medium size, near your farm Mid Game $500K–$1.5M Buy 2–3 more fields, prioritize fields near your existing ones Late Game $1.5M+ Buy remaining fields; consider buying entire farms or large plots

docs.title: What to Look for in a Field
docs.text:
1. Proximity to your farm — The closer, the less time you waste driving equipment between jobs. Fields adjacent to your current land are ideal.
  2. Shape — Rectangular fields are more efficient and AI-worker-friendly. Avoid triangular or L-shaped fields until you have experience.
  3. Size — Small fields cost less but require disproportionate time for setup and transport. Medium fields (1–5 ha) offer the best balance.
  4. Soil type — Some fields have better base fertility. Check the soil map before buying.
  5. Access — Does the field have a road frontage? Can you get equipment to it easily?

docs.title: Typical Land Prices
docs.text:
Field Size Price Range Example Small (under 1 ha) $20K–$80K A 0.5 ha plot near town Medium (1–5 ha) $80K–$300K A 3 ha rectangular field Large (5–10 ha) $300K–$700K A 7 ha farm field Very Large (10+ ha) $700K–$2M+ A 15 ha field with buildings Entire farm property $1M–$5M+ A complete farm with buildings and multiple fields

Tip: You can buy individual fields without buying the entire farm. Check the land tab in the menu (P key) to see which plots are available and their exact boundaries on the map.

docs.title: 🏗️ Farm Setup & Building Placement
docs.text: Where you place your buildings, production facilities, and animal pens is critical to long-term efficiency. Bad placement can cost you hours of extra driving.

docs.title: Essential Buildings and Where to Place Them
docs.text: Building Recommended Location Why Farmhouse Near your first fields Respawn point — you'll come here often Vehicle shed Central to your fields Protects equipment from weather damage Silo / Grain storage Near vehicle shed Minimize grain hauling distance Fuel station Near vehicle shed Fuel up before heading to fields Workshop Near vehicle shed Repair equipment quickly Animal pens On flat ground, near grain fields Easy access to feed Production buildings Near your farm or town Convenient for selling processed goods

docs.title: The Central Hub Principle
docs.text: Place your main farm hub as close to the center of your fields as possible. Each second of extra drive time per trip adds up over hundreds of trips. On Riverbend Springs, the starting farm is already well-positioned. On Zielonka, you may want to buy a field with a building for a second hub.

docs.title: 📋 Tips for Efficient Field Layout
docs.text:
1. Group your fields — When buying new fields, prioritize those adjacent to your existing ones. A contiguous block of land is dramatically more efficient than scattered plots.

  2. Remove unnecessary hedges and fences — Use the landscaping tool to clear hedgerows between fields you own. Combine small adjacent fields into larger, more efficient ones.

  3. Leave headland space — When combining fields, leave room at the edges for turning. AI workers need space to turn around cleanly.

  4. Create field access lanes — Use the landscaping tool to create gravel paths between your main farm road and field entrances. Mud slows equipment down.

  5. Consider crop rotation across fields — Plant different crops in different fields to spread harvest times. This prevents a situation where all your fields are ready at once and you can't keep up.

  6. Use grass fields strategically — Dedicate one or two fields to grass for silage production. Grass requires minimal maintenance and silage is extremely profitable through the biogas plant.

  7. Plan for expansion — When setting up your first farm, leave room for future buildings. Nothing is worse than wanting to add a dairy barn and discovering your vehicle shed is in the way.

Tip: On Riverbend Springs, the cluster of fields around the starting farm (fields 1–8) makes an excellent first expansion area. Buying them one by one creates a contiguous empire of prime farmland.

docs.title: Riverbend Springs
docs.text:
• Best expansion path: Fields 1→2→3→4 (north and east of starting farm)
  • Best selling point: The grain elevator north of town offers consistently good prices
  • Hidden gem: The small forest plot southeast of field 12 is a great starter forestry area

docs.title: Zielonka
docs.text:
• Best expansion path: Buy the farm with fields 7–12 first; it comes with buildings
  • Watch out for: Narrow bridges — don't buy the biggest combine until you know you can cross
  • Hidden gem: The biogas plant is well-positioned for silage operations

docs.title: Hutan Pantai
docs.text:
• Best expansion path: Focus on rice fields near water sources first
  • Consider: Keeping equipment small — large Western tractors don't fit on the terraces
  • Hidden gem: The mountain forest offers excellent timber with minimal competition

Ready to expand your farming operation? Check out the Profit Guide for detailed crop-by-crop earnings analysis, or the Machinery Guide for help choosing the right equipment for your fields.

docs.title: 🏭 Farming Simulator 25 — Production Chains Guide
docs.text: Processing raw crops into finished goods is how you go from broke farmer to agri-empire. This guide breaks down every production chain, its profit margin per step, factory placement strategy, and advanced optimization techniques.

docs.title: 📊 Complete Production Chain Profit Table
docs.text:
All figures per 1,000 L of input. Prices are mid-range — actuals fluctuate with market demand.

Chain Step 1 Value Added Step 2 Value Added Total Added 🌾 Wheat → Flour → Bread Wheat ($260) → Flour ($550) +$290 Flour ($550) + Water → Bread ($1,250) +$700 +$990 🌻 Canola → Oil Canola ($610) → Canola Oil ($1,800) +$1,190 — — +$1,190 🌻 Sunflower → Oil Sunflower ($610) → Sunflower Oil ($1,700) +$1,090 — — +$1,090 🧅 Sugar Beet → Sugar Sugar Beet ($210) → Sugar ($1,400) +$1,190 — — +$1,190 🍇 Grapes → Juice → Wine Grapes ($2,200) → Grape Juice ($3,960) +$1,760 Juice ($3,960) + Aging → Wine ($8,800) +$4,840 +$6,600 🧵 Cotton → Fabric → Clothing Cotton ($1,430) → Fabric ($3,640) +$2,210 Fabric ($3,640) → Clothing ($6,500) +$2,860 +$5,070 🥔 Potatoes → Chips Potatoes ($2,020) → Chips ($5,200) +$3,180 — — +$3,180 🥛 Milk → Cheese → Butter Milk ($800) → Cheese ($2,400) +$1,600 Milk ($800) → Butter ($3,200) +$2,400 +$2,400 🌽 Corn → Silage → Biogas Corn ($504) → Silage ($1,100) +$596 Silage → Biogas ($3,000/yr) +$1,900/yr Recurring

💡 Key Insight: The Grapes → Juice → Wine chain has the highest value-add per liter in the game. A single vineyard + winery setup can generate $100K+/month.

docs.title: 💰 Profit Margin per Step: Raw vs Processed
Grain Chain: Wheat → Flour → Bread
docs.text:
Product Sell Price/1,000L Time to Produce Profit/hr (1 plant) Multiplier 🌾 Raw Wheat $260 0 (harvest) — 1.0x 🌾 Flour $550 20 min $870 2.1x 🍞 Bread $1,250 32 min (total) $928 4.8x

Verdict: Selling raw wheat is a 1x mistake. Flour doubles value. Bread nearly quintuples it.

docs.title: Canola & Sunflower → Oil Chain
docs.text:
Product Sell Price/1,000L Time to Produce Profit/hr Multiplier 🌻 Raw Canola $610 — — 1.0x 🛢️ Canola Oil $1,800 15 min $2,380 2.95x

Verdict: Oil presses have the fastest ROI of any processor. A canola field + oil mill pays for itself in 2 seasons.

docs.title: Grapes → Juice → Wine Chain
docs.text:
Product Sell Price/1,000L Time to Produce Profit/hr Multiplier 🍇 Raw Grapes $2,200 — — 1.0x 🧃 Grape Juice $3,960 25 min $2,112 1.8x 🍷 Wine $8,800 45 min + aging $4,400+ 4.0x

⚠️ Note: Wine requires aging time (in-game hours) before it reaches peak quality and price. Plan your wine cellar capacity accordingly.

docs.title: Sugar Beet → Sugar Chain
docs.text:
Product Sell Price/1,000L Time to Produce Profit/hr Multiplier 🧅 Raw Sugar Beet $210 — — 1.0x 🍬 Sugar $1,400 18 min $2,367 6.7x

Verdict: Sugar from sugar beets has the highest multiplier (6.7x) of any single-step chain. If you have a sugar mill, prioritize sugar beets over every other field crop.

docs.title: Production Chain Factory Locations
docs.text: Factory Best Map Proximity Strategy Purchase Cost 🌾 Grain Mill (Flour) Riverbend Springs Near wheat fields and water $85,000 🛢️ Oil Mill Hutan Pantai Center of map, near canola/sunflower $120,000 🍞 Bakery Riverbend Springs Near grain mill (short wheat/flour haul) $95,000 🍷 Winery Zielonka Vineyard-adjacent, hillside position $180,000 🍬 Sugar Mill Riverbend Springs Near sugar beet fields $110,000 🧵 Spinning Mill Hutan Pantai Near cotton fields, flat terrain $130,000 🧀 Dairy Riverbend Springs Central, between pastures and town $75,000 🏭 Biogas Plant Any Near corn/silage fields, edge of map $500,000

docs.title: Best Map for Production Chains: Riverbend Springs 🌟
docs.text:
Riverbend Springs is the undisputed best map for production chains because:

  1. Central location — All factories can be placed within 500m of each other
  2. Flat terrain — Easier trailer logistics, fewer rollovers
  3. Pre-existing fields — Large field sizes (F1–F10) near water
  4. Short road to sell points — Bakery goods go to the restaurant in town
  5. Water access — Grain mill requires water proximity

Runner-up: Hutan Pantai offers larger contiguous fields but longer road distances. Zielonka is best for vineyard-centric strategies.

docs.title: ⏱️ Time Investment: Manual vs AI Worker Processing
docs.text:
Task Manual Time (10ha) AI Worker Time AI Cost Best For 🌾 Harvest wheat 25 min 40 min $1,200 Let AI do it 🚜 Transport to processor 15 min N/A (you drive) — Always manual 🏭 Load processor 5 min N/A — Quick manual 🍷 Collect wine product 2 min N/A — Quick manual 📦 Deliver to sell point 12 min N/A — Always manual 🌽 Silage harvesting 30 min 50 min $2,500 AI for first pass 🧑‍🌾 Fertilizing 20 min 25 min $800 AI is cheaper

Strategy: Use AI workers ONLY for field work (cultivating, seeding, fertilizing, harvesting). Always handle transport, processing, and selling yourself — those are high-value, low-time tasks.

docs.title: 🌦️ Seasonal Considerations
docs.text: Chain Best Season Worst Season Seasonality Strategy 🌾 Grain → Flour → Bread Harvest (Jul–Aug) Winter Process in fall/winter when prices are higher 🌻 Canola/Sunflower → Oil Post-harvest (Aug–Sep) Spring Store oil, sell before next harvest dip 🍇 Grapes → Juice → Wine Harvest (Aug–Sep) Winter Wine improves with aging; sell next summer 🧅 Sugar Beet → Sugar Harvest (Sep–Oct) Spring Sugar doesn't spoil — hold for best price 🧵 Cotton → Fabric Harvest (Sep–Oct) Spring Fabric prices stable year-round 🥛 Dairy processing Year-round None Steady daily income, no seasonality

docs.title: Year-Round Production Strategy
docs.text:
Production Type Input Source Winter Viable? Requires 🏠 Greenhouse vegetables Indoor planting ✅ Yes Heated greenhouse (FS25) 🐄 Dairy barn Hay, silage (stored) ✅ Yes Barn heater + stored feed 🧀 Cheese/Butter Milk from barn ✅ Yes Dairy + barns 🏭 Oil mill Stored canola/sunflower ✅ Yes Adequate silo storage 🍞 Bakery Stored flour ✅ Yes Grain mill + flour storage 🌽 Biogas Corn silage (stored) ✅ Yes Silage bunker + biogas plant

Tip: Build at least 2M L of silo storage before winter. Process throughout the cold months when field work is impossible — this turns idle time into $40K–$80K/month.

docs.title: Quality Bonuses from Precision Farming
docs.text:
With the Precision Farming DLC, fertilizer quality directly impacts processing yields:

Fertilization Level Yield Bonus Processing Output Bonus Total Chain Profit Impact 0x (No fertilizer) Base 100% Base 100% Baseline 1x Fertilized +15% yield +5% processing output +9–15% chain profit 2x Fertilized +30% yield +10% processing output +20–28% chain profit 3x (Perfect) +50% yield +15% processing output +33–42% chain profit

docs.title: Optimal Fertilizer Strategy per Chain
docs.text: Chain Recommended Fertilization Why 🌾 Wheat → Bread 3x perfect High-value end product; every quality point multiplies 🍇 Grapes → Wine 2x (optimal) Grapes are perennial; third application has diminishing returns 🌻 Canola → Oil 2x (cost-effective) Oil mill already doubles value; extra fert is lower priority 🧅 Sugar Beet → Sugar 2x Sugar beet already high yield; 3rd pass marginal 🧵 Cotton → Fabric 3x perfect Fabric price high enough to justify perfect fert 🥔 Potatoes → Chips 2x Chips already 2.5x multiplier; fert at 2x is sweet spot

docs.title: Integrated Farm Strategy
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Field Crops] --> B[Harvest & Store]
    B --> C{Process or Sell?}
    C -->|Sell raw| D[Immediate cash - low profit]
    C -->|Process| E[Factory]
    E --> F[Store finished goods]
    F --> G{Sell timing?}
    G -->|Off-peak demand| H[Wait for high price]
    G -->|Need cash| I[Sell at current price]
    H --> J[MAX PROFIT]
    I --> K[Lower profit but liquid]
    A --> L[Fertilize 2x min]
    L --> A

Pro Strategy: Never sell raw crops after Year 1. By Year 2, every field should feed into at least one processing chain. The Grapes→Wine chain alone can generate $300K+/year from a single hectare of vineyard.

Data source: Farming Simulator 25 gameplay data, community testing, precision farming DLC mechanics.

docs.title: Farming Simulator 25 — Complete Profit Guide
docs.text: Understanding crop profitability is the foundation of a successful farm in Farming Simulator 25. This guide breaks down every major crop by revenue, cost, net profit per hectare, growing time, and seasonal considerations to help you maximize your bottom line.

docs.title: Crop Profitability Table
docs.text:
All figures are per hectare (ha) and assume average yield with standard fertilization (no precision farming bonuses). Prices fluctuate based on market demand — the values below represent typical mid-range prices.

Crop Revenue/ha Seed Cost/ha Fertilizer Cost/ha Net Profit/ha Growth Time Harvest Months Wheat $2,200 $150 $200 $1,850 6 days Jul–Aug Barley $2,100 $140 $200 $1,760 6 days Jun–Jul Oats $2,000 $130 $200 $1,670 5 days Jun–Jul Canola $3,000 $240 $250 $2,510 7 days Jul–Aug Sunflower $2,800 $220 $250 $2,330 7 days Aug–Sep Soybeans $3,800 $300 $250 $3,250 8 days Aug–Sep Corn (Maize) $4,200 $350 $300 $3,550 9 days Sep–Oct Potatoes $6,500 $800 $400 $5,300 8 days Aug–Sep Sugar Beet $7,200 $900 $400 $5,900 9 days Sep–Oct Cotton $5,500 $600 $350 $4,550 10 days Sep–Oct Sorghum $2,400 $170 $200 $2,030 6 days Jul–Aug Rice (Paddy) $4,800 $500 $350 $3,950 10 days Sep–Oct Grapes $9,000 $2,000 $500 $6,500 Perennial Aug–Sep Olives $8,000 $1,800 $500 $5,700 Perennial Sep–Oct Poplar $3,200 $400 N/A $2,800 12 months All year

docs.title: Profit per Day Analysis
docs.text:
When planning your crop rotation, profit per day (net profit ÷ growth time) gives the truest picture of efficiency.

Crop Net Profit/ha Growth Days Profit/Day Sugar Beet $5,900 9 $655 Potatoes $5,300 8 $663 Corn $3,550 9 $394 Rice $3,950 10 $395 Soybeans $3,250 8 $406 Grapes $6,500 Perennial ~$300/day (over 22 months) Canola $2,510 7 $359 Cotton $4,550 10 $455 Sunflower $2,330 7 $333 Wheat $1,850 6 $308 Barley $1,760 6 $293

Top 3 by Profit/Day: Potatoes ($663), Sugar Beet ($655), Cotton ($455).

docs.title: Crop Products & Processing Profit
docs.text: Processing raw crops into higher-value products dramatically increases profitability. Below are the most lucrative processing chains.

docs.title: Grains & Flour
docs.text: Product Raw Input Output Value Value Added Flour 100 L Wheat ($55) $110 +$55 Bread 100 L Flour ($110) + 20 L Water $250 +$140 Cake 100 L Flour + 50 L Milk + 30 L Eggs $380 +$230 Oat Flakes 100 L Oats ($50) $120 +$70

docs.title: Sunflower & Canola → Oil
docs.text: Product Raw Input Output Value Value Added Canola Oil 100 L Canola ($75) $180 +$105 Sunflower Oil 100 L Sunflowers ($70) $170 +$100

docs.title: Sugar Beet → Sugar
docs.text: Product Raw Input Output Value Value Added Sugar 100 L Sugar Beets ($40) $140 +$100 Cake (uses Sugar) 100 L Sugar + other inputs $380 Variable

docs.title: Grapes → Juice → Wine
docs.text: Product Raw Input Output Value Value Added Grape Juice 100 L Grapes ($200) $360 +$160 Wine 100 L Grape Juice ($360) + aging $800 +$440

docs.title: Cotton → Fabric
docs.text: Product Raw Input Output Value Value Added Fabric 100 L Cotton ($120) $280 +$160 Clothing 100 L Fabric ($280) $500 +$220

docs.title: Seasonal Considerations
docs.text:
Farming Simulator 25 features a dynamic seasonal cycle that affects crop viability.

Season Best Crops to Plant Notes Spring (Mar–May) Wheat, Barley, Oats, Canola, Potatoes Prepare fields, fertilize before planting Summer (Jun–Aug) Corn, Soybeans, Sunflowers, Sorghum Fast growth, good weather windows Fall (Sep–Nov) Harvest most crops, plant winter wheat/barley Busiest season — plan equipment carefully Winter (Dec–Feb) Animal care, maintenance, logging Minimal field work; focus on animals and processing

docs.title: Rotation Strategy
docs.text:
A 3-field rotation maximizing profit:

  • Field 1: Potatoes (Spring) → Harvest (Aug) → Winter Wheat (Sep)
  • Field 2: Corn (Spring) → Harvest (Sep) → Fallow/Green Manure
  • Field 3: Soybeans (Spring) → Harvest (Aug) → Canola (Winter)

docs.title: Cost-Saving Tips
docs.text:
1. Fertilize efficiently — use precision farming to apply variable-rate fertilizer only where needed, saving 20–30% on input costs.
  2. Own your equipment — leasing is great for specialized harvesters, but buying tractors and basic tools pays off after 3 seasons.
  3. Sell at peak prices — crop prices fluctuate weekly. Store grain in silos and sell when prices are 15–20% above average.
  4. Use cover crops — planting grass or clover between cash crops reduces fertilizer needs by 50%.
  5. Lime management — test soil pH and apply lime only when required. Over-liming wastes money without additional yield benefit.
  6. Weed control — mechanical weeding (hoeing) costs less than herbicide and doesn't degrade soil quality.

docs.title: Animal Profitability
docs.text:
If you diversify into animal husbandry, here are the profit estimates per animal per year:

Animal Initial Cost Annual Profit Products Chicken $150 each $1,200/yr Eggs, Meat Sheep $500 each $2,500/yr Wool, Meat Cow (Dairy) $1,200 each $4,000/yr Milk, Meat Pig $400 each $3,000/yr Meat, Manure Horse $3,000 each $500/yr Breeding (harder to profit)

Best Animal Investment: Dairy cows offer the highest annual return, but require the most investment in barns and feeding equipment. Chickens are the easiest entry point with the lowest startup cost.

docs.title: 📊 Crop vs Animal ROI Comparison
docs.text:
Which earns more — planting crops or raising animals? Here's the per-hectare equivalent comparison.

Enterprise Startup Cost (per unit) Annual Profit (per unit) Land Needed Labor Risk Level ROI Year 1 🌾 Wheat (1 ha) $350 (seed+fert) $1,850 1 ha Low Low 528% 🌻 Sunflowers (1 ha) $470 $2,330 1 ha Low Low 495% 🌽 Corn (1 ha) $650 $3,550 1 ha Medium Medium 546% 🥔 Potatoes (1 ha) $1,200 $5,300 1 ha High Medium 441% 🍇 Grapes (1 ha) $2,500 $6,500 1 ha Medium Low (perennial) 260% 🐔 Chickens (10) $1,500 (coop+birds) $1,200/yr Tiny Very Low Low 80% 🐑 Sheep (5) $5,000 (pasture+sheep) $2,500/yr 0.5 ha Low Low 50% 🐄 Dairy Cows (5) $15,000 (barn+cows) $4,000/yr 1 ha (feed) High Medium 27% 🐖 Pigs (5) $7,000 (pen+pigs) $3,000/yr 0.5 ha Medium Medium 43%

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text: Crops give faster ROI but require seasonal work. Animals provide steady daily income but need higher upfront investment. Start with crops, add chickens for daily cash flow, then scale to cows as you build feed production.

docs.title: 💼 Contract Work Income Table
docs.text:
Contracts are a great early-game money source. Here's what each type pays.

Contract Type Avg Payout Time Needed Hourly Rate Equipment Provided? Verdict 🌾 Harvest (small field) $3,000–$5,000 10–15 min $12,000–$20,000/hr ✅ Yes (harvester + trailer) ⭐⭐⭐ Best starter 🌾 Harvest (medium field) $8,000–$15,000 20–40 min $12,000–$22,500/hr ✅ Yes ⭐⭐⭐⭐ 🌾 Harvest (large field) $20,000–$40,000 60–90 min $15,000–$26,000/hr ✅ Yes ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 🚜 Cultivate $2,000–$8,000 15–30 min $8,000–$16,000/hr ✅ Yes ⭐⭐⭐ 🧑‍🌾 Sowing/Planting $2,500–$10,000 15–40 min $6,000–$15,000/hr ✅ Yes ⭐⭐ 🪵 Logging $5,000–$20,000 30–60 min $5,000–$20,000/hr ✅ Yes ⭐⭐ (requires skill) 🧹 Baling $3,000–$12,000 20–45 min $6,000–$16,000/hr ✅ Yes ⭐⭐⭐ 🥔 Potato Harvest $10,000–$25,000 30–60 min $10,000–$25,000/hr ✅ Yes ⭐⭐⭐⭐ 🐄 Transport Animals $1,500–$4,000 5–10 min $12,000–$24,000/hr ✅ Trailer ⭐⭐ (one-off) 🌾 Weeding/Spraying $2,000–$6,000 10–25 min $8,000–$14,400/hr ✅ Yes ⭐⭐⭐

docs.title: Contract Strategy
docs.text:
Phase Strategy Expected Income 🔰 Day 1–5 Do all harvest contracts — highest pay, equipment included $15K–$30K/day 🟢 Week 2–3 Keep doing harvest + cultivate; buy your own tractor + trailer $30K–$60K/day 🟡 Month 2+ Be selective — only take contracts near your fields $50K–$100K/day 🔴 Late game Skip contracts — your own farm earns more per hour N/A

💡 Pro tip: Accept a contract, then use the leased equipment for your own fields too! The rental fee is already covered by the contract.

docs.title: 🏆 Best Investment Order (Expanded)
docs.text: Priority Investment Cost Monthly Return Payback Period Alternative 1 🚜 Medium Tractor (150 HP) $80K +50% field speed Immediate Rent first if low cash 2 🌾 Seeder + Planter $65K +100% planting speed 2 seasons Rent for first season 3 🗺️ Buy 2nd field (small) $100K $3K–$5K/month 8–12 months Lease field if cheaper 4 🚛 Larger trailer (26K L) $55K Fewer trips = more $ 3–4 seasons Skip if solo farming 5 🐔 Chicken coop (10 hens) $1.5K $100/month 15 months Best early passive income 6 🌾 Combine harvester $250K Save $2K/rental × ~20 uses 6–8 years rent savings Keep renting until 5+ fields 7 🧑‍🌾 Fertilizer spreader + sprayer $60K +30% yield on all fields 2–3 seasons Rent first 8 🐄 Dairy barn + 5 cows $20K $1.5K/month 13 months Start with chickens first 9 🏗️ Grain dryer (corn/rice) $75K Premium prices for dried grain 3–4 seasons Only needed for certain crops 10 🏭 Oil mill / Flour mill $150K Double crop value 5–8 seasons Game-changer when processing 11 🗺️ Buy larger fields $200K+ Scales all profit 3–6 months Always a good investment 12 🏗️ Biogas plant $500K $5K–$10K/month 4–8 years Only after 50+ cows or 100+ acres 13 🏪 Farm shop / Direct sales $100K +30% sell price 4–6 seasons Best late-game upgrade

docs.title: Early Game Priority (First $500K)
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Start] --> B[Medium Tractor]
    B --> C[Seed & Fertilize]
    C --> D[Buy 2nd Field]
    D --> E[Harvester - Rent]
    E --> F[Chickens for passive $]
    F --> G[Grain Dryer or Oil Mill]
    G --> H[Expand to 5+ Fields]

docs.title: Mid-Game Priority ($500K–$1.5M)
docs.text:
1. Own a combine harvester — stop renting, keep full harvest profit
  2. Build processing chain — oil press for canola/sunflower, flour mill for wheat
  3. Add dairy cows (5–10 head) — steady milk income
  4. Buy precision farming gear — variable-rate fertilizer saves 20–30%
  5. Expand to 10+ fields — scale is the ultimate profit multiplier

docs.title: Late Game ($1.5M+)
docs.text:
1. Biogas plant — turns silage into $10K+/month passive income
  2. Farm shop — sell processed goods at retail prices
  3. Greenhouses — year-round vegetable production, minimal labor
  4. Buy every field on the map — total farmland dominance

Data source: Farming Simulator Wiki 1.0

docs.title: Purchasing Tips
docs.text:
1. Match tractor power to implement requirements — every tool has a minimum HP requirement. Undersized tractors work slowly; oversized tractors waste fuel.
  2. Consider fuel efficiency — Fendt tractors generally consume less fuel per HP/hour than competitors.
  3. Speed matters — a 50 km/h tractor covers fields faster than a 40 km/h model, saving real time on large maps.
  4. Don't overbuy — start with a medium tractor (140–150 HP) and a mid-range combine. Upgrade as your operation grows.
  5. Used vehicles — check the used vehicle dealer regularly for substantial discounts on older models.
  6. Lease before you buy — leasing a vehicle for a single contract is often cheaper than purchasing for infrequent use.

Data source: Farming Simulator Wiki 1.0

docs.title: Fields of Mistria — Complete Game Overview
About the Game
docs.text: Fields of Mistria is a farming simulation RPG developed by NPC Studio, released in August 2024 via Early Access on Steam. Set in a charming pastoral town recovering from a mysterious earthquake, players inherit a run-down farm and work to restore both the land and the nearby village.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text: Feature Details 🎮 Genre Farming Sim / RPG / Life Sim 👥 Mode Single-player 🏭 Developer NPC Studio 📅 Release August 2024 (Early Access) 🖥️ Platform PC (Steam)

docs.title: 🪄 Magic & Skills
docs.text: Unlike traditional farming sims, Fields of Mistria introduces a magic system that evolves alongside your farming skills. Players learn spells tied to seasonal elements — water magic for irrigating crops, earth magic for tilling, and fire magic for smithing. The magic system ties directly into skill progression, with higher levels unlocking more powerful enchantments for tools and crops.

docs.title: Skill Categories
docs.text:
• 🌱 Farming — Plant, water, and harvest crops; magic-enhanced yields
  • ⛏️ Mining — Explore the mines beneath Mistria, collect ores and gems
  • 🎣 Fishing — Catch freshwater and ocean fish in various biomes
  • 🌿 Foraging — Gather wild ingredients and discover rare herbs
  • 🧙 Magic — Unlock and upgrade spells through practice and quests

docs.title: 🏘️ Town & NPCs
docs.text:
Mistria is home to a cast of fully voiced NPCs with deep relationship mechanics. Each villager has daily routines, seasonal events, and personal story arcs tied to the town's recovery. Building friendships unlocks new spells, recipes, and story progression.

Tip: Spend your first few days clearing only enough land to plant a small crop patch. Use the rest of your energy exploring town and talking to villagers — early relationships unlock important tool upgrades and magic abilities faster than rushing farm expansion.

docs.title: 🐄 Fields of Mistria — Animals & Pets Ultimate Guide
docs.text: Ranching is your most reliable daily income source in the mid-to-late game. Unlike crops, animals don't need replanting every day, and product quality scales linearly with friendship. Smart barn management is key to financial freedom.

docs.title: 🐔 All Purchasable Animals
docs.text:
Animal Cost Building Product Base Price Daily Profit Interval 🐔 Chicken 800g Coop Egg🥚 50g 38g Daily 🦆 Duck 2000g Coop (Upgraded) Duck Egg🥚 80g 48g Daily 🐄 Cow 3000g Barn Milk🥛 100g 70g Daily 🐑 Sheep 4000g Barn (Upgraded) Wool🧶 150g 55g Every 2 days 🐐 Goat 5000g Barn (Large) Goat Milk🥛 180g 125g Daily 🐇 Rabbit 3000g Coop (Large) Rabbit Fur🧶 200g 50g Every 3 days 🦙 Alpaca 8000g Barn (Large) Alpaca Wool🧶 280g 110g Every 2 days 🐖 Pig 12000g Barn (Deluxe) Truffle🍄 350g 175g Daily (Sunny)

Tip: Pigs have the highest daily profit (175g), but no truffles on rainy days. With 6–8 rainy days per month on average, real daily profit is ~130g. Goats are the true daily profit king — 125g every day, rain or shine.

docs.title: ❤️ Friendship System & Milestones
docs.text:
Hearts Days (Pet + Feed Daily) Unlock ❤️ 1 Heart 0–3 days Basic quality products ❤️❤️ 2 Hearts 4–7 days Small coop auto-feeder unlocked ❤️❤️❤️ 3 Hearts 8–12 days Silver quality products (1.25× price) ❤️❤️❤️❤️ 4 Hearts 13–18 days Large barn auto-feeder + Animal festival entry ❤️❤️❤️❤️❤️ 5 Hearts 19–25 days Gold quality products (1.5× price) ⭐ Max Friendship 26–35 days Iridium quality products (2× price) + Special products

Tip: It takes about 30 days to max out one animal. Petting daily is the most efficient method — same 2 seconds, twice the reward of feeding. Starting with 4 chickens takes just 10 seconds of morning work for 150g+ daily income.

docs.title: 🐾 Pet System
docs.text:
Pet How to Get Special Ability Verdict 🐱 Cat Pet Shop 2000g Random daily gift (rare fish/gems possible) 🥇 Practical 🐶 Dog Pet Shop 2000g Herding — automatically brings grazing animals inside 🥇 Lazy farmer's best friend 🦊 Fox Forest event trigger Finds hidden forage items (night bonus) 🥈 Forager build 🐦 Owl Complete wizard tower quest Night pest control — protects stored grain 🥈 Stockpile build 🐢 Turtle Beach 4000g Auto-collects pond products 🥉 Fishing build

Pet Friendship Upgrades (ability doubles at 5 hearts): - Cat: Better gift rarity, may bring gems - Dog: Herding range expands from 5 tiles to 15 tiles - Fox: Forage markers appear on the minimap

docs.title: 🏠 Barn Upgrades & Auto-Feeders
docs.text:
Building Capacity Cost/Materials Auto-Feed Basic Coop 4 2000g + 40 Wood + 20 Stone ❌ Large Coop 8 6000g + 60 Wood + 40 Stone + 5 Iron Bars ✅ Deluxe Coop 12 12000g + 10 Gold Bars + 8 Iron Bars + 80 Wood ✅ Basic Barn 4 3000g + 50 Wood + 30 Stone ❌ Large Barn 8 8000g + 80 Wood + 50 Stone + 8 Iron Bars ✅ Deluxe Barn 12 18000g + 15 Gold Bars + 10 Iron Bars + 100 Wood + 5 Silk ✅

Auto-Feeder ROI: An auto-feeder saves ~3 minutes of hay-placing per day. At an opportunity cost of ~300g/hour, the 6000g Large Barn upgrade pays for itself in about 13 days. Upgrade before buying your 3rd animal.

docs.title: ⭐ Product Quality Tiers & Improvement
docs.text:
Quality Price Multiplier Requirement How to Improve Normal ⚪ 1.0× Default — Silver ⭐ 1.25× 3 Hearts Daily petting + Pasture grazing Gold ⭐⭐ 1.5× 5 Hearts Premium feed + Magic grass seeds Iridium ⭐⭐⭐ 2.0× Max Friendship + Magic Collar Complete wizard tower quest, then buy the collar

Iridium Strategy: One iridium goat produces 360g of milk per day (180×2) — equivalent to 3 regular chickens combined. Focus on raising 3 high-friendship goats instead of 12 regular chickens. Recommended squad: 2 Cows (cheese ingredient) + 4 Goats (cash flow) + 2 Alpacas (premium cloth).

docs.title: ❄️ Seasonal Strategy & Winter Prep
docs.text:
Season Grazing Possible? Notes 🌸 Spring ✅ Graze Plant grass starters — 1 tile of grass feeds 1 animal ☀️ Summer ✅ Graze Fastest grass growth — stockpile hay (use scythe) 🍂 Fall ✅ Graze until the 20th Last 8 days — start hoarding hay for winter ❄️ Winter ❌ Indoors only 2 hay per animal per day — stockpile 50+ bales

Winter Hay Budget: 8 animals × 2 hay/day × 28 days = 448 hay bales. During the last two weeks of fall, use your scythe on grass (3–5 hay per swing); every patch of grass you preserve = 10–15 bales in reserve.

Tip: Think winter means no animal products? Wrong! Indoor animals produce normally in winter — you just can't graze them. Animals at 3+ hearts still produce Silver quality in winter. Winter is actually the best time to grind friendship — just pop inside for a pet and you're done, no mining time lost.

docs.title: 📊 Ranching vs. Farming Profit Comparison
docs.text:
Metric 🌱 Crops (20 Plots) 🐄 Ranching (4 Goats) Daily Income 180–250g (season-dependent) 500g (stable) Upfront Cost 200–800g seeds 12000g + barn Daily Labor Water 20 times 2 minutes Payback Period 4–10 days 24 days Upgrade Potential Magic enhancements Iridium ×2 multiplier

Bottom Line: Spring crops build capital → Summer barn expansion → Fall goat purchases → Winter ranching cash flow + greenhouse strawberry cooking. Dual-track for maximum profit.

Data source: Fields of Mistria v0.8+ (Early Access)

docs.title: 🏭 Fields of Mistria — Complete Artisan Processing & Endgame Profit Model
docs.text:
Data version: Fields of Mistria v1.0 (current) | Sources: Fields of Mistria Wiki, in-game testing | Last verified: 2026-07-06

Fields of Mistria's economy runs on processing. A 20g potato becomes 130g preserves (+550%). A 60g tomato becomes 150g preserves (+150%). But unlike Stardew Valley where Kegs are the universal answer, Mistria forces you to make trade-offs — you only get one processing machine type per crop, and your Perk tree choices dramatically change which routes are optimal.

This guide breaks down every processing machine, every crop's best route, and the optimal endgame setup for three player tiers.

docs.title: 📖 Artisan Mechanics Overview
docs.text:
Fields of Mistria has three main processing categories for crops:

Category Machine Unlock Input Output Base Time 🫙 Preserves Preserves Jar Town Rank 1 (Farming) Vegetables & Fruits Pickles / Jams ~6 hrs 🥤 Juice Juice Press Town Rank 2 (Farming) Vegetables & Fruits Juices / Extracts ~4 hrs 🥗 Cooking Kitchen (House Upgrade) Upgrade 2 Varied ingredients Prepared dishes Instant 🧀 Animal Artisan Various Barn upgrades Milk, Eggs, Wool Cheese, Mayo, Cloth 3-6 hrs 🎣 Fish Processing Smoker / Pickler Town Rank 3 Fish Smoked / Pickled Fish ~8 hrs

Key difference from Stardew: Fields of Mistria's processing times are faster (hours, not days) and there is no Keg — the Juice Press is the closest equivalent. Also, cooking is genuinely profitable here unlike in most farm sims, because dishes can be sold for a multiple of their ingredient cost.

docs.title: 🫙 Preserves Jar — The Workhorse
docs.text: Unlocked at Town Rank 1 (Farming), the Preserves Jar is your first processing machine and remains relevant all game. It takes ~6 hours to process one input.

docs.title: All Preserves Jar Recipes
docs.text:
Input Base Price Preserves Price Profit Multiplier Profit/Hour 🥔 Potato 50g 130g +80g ×2.6 13.3g/h 🥕 Carrot 46g 120g +74g ×2.6 12.3g/h 🥬 Cabbage 75g 175g +100g ×2.3 16.7g/h 🥦 Broccoli 100g 225g +125g ×2.3 20.8g/h 🥦 Cauliflower 135g 290g +155g ×2.1 25.8g/h 🍓 Strawberry 85g 200g +115g ×2.4 19.2g/h 🫘 Green Bean 45g 115g +70g ×2.6 11.7g/h 🍅 Tomato 60g 150g +90g ×2.5 15.0g/h 🌽 Corn 70g 165g +95g ×2.4 15.8g/h 🫑 Pepper 50g 130g +80g ×2.6 13.3g/h 🍉 Watermelon 160g 340g +180g ×2.1 30.0g/h 🫐 Blueberry 75g 180g +105g ×2.4 17.5g/h 🍆 Eggplant 65g 160g +95g ×2.5 15.8g/h 🌻 Sunflower 40g 110g +70g ×2.8 11.7g/h 🍈 Melon 140g 300g +160g ×2.1 26.7g/h 🎃 Pumpkin 150g 320g +170g ×2.1 28.3g/h 🍠 Sweet Potato 55g 135g +80g ×2.5 13.3g/h 🥬 Beetroot 50g 125g +75g ×2.5 12.5g/h 🫐 Cranberry 85g 210g +125g ×2.5 20.8g/h 🥬 Kale 66g 155g +89g ×2.3 14.8g/h 🥬 Turnip 36g 100g +64g ×2.8 10.7g/h 🌾 Rice 90g 200g +110g ×2.2 18.3g/h 🌿 Artichoke 110g 245g +135g ×2.2 22.5g/h 🌸 Amaranth 42g 110g +68g ×2.6 11.3g/h 🧶 Cotton 55g 135g +80g ×2.5 13.3g/h

🏆 Best tesserae/hour in Preserves Jar: Watermelon (30.0g/h) → Pumpkin (28.3g/h) → Melon (26.7g/h) → Cauliflower (25.8g/h)

docs.title: 🥤 Juice Press — Faster Turnover
docs.text:
Unlocked at Town Rank 2 (Farming). The Juice Press processes in ~4 hours — faster than the Preserves Jar but with lower per-unit markup. Its key advantage: higher profit per hour despite lower markup, because it cycles faster.

Input Base Price Juice Price Profit Multiplier Profit/Hour 🥕 Carrot 46g 115g +69g ×2.5 17.3g/h 🌽 Corn 70g 165g +95g ×2.4 23.8g/h 🥬 Kale 66g 155g +89g ×2.3 22.3g/h 🥬 Beetroot 50g 125g +75g ×2.5 18.8g/h 🥔 Potato 50g 120g +70g ×2.4 17.5g/h 🍉 Watermelon 160g 330g +170g ×2.1 42.5g/h 🔥 🍈 Melon 140g 290g +150g ×2.1 37.5g/h 🎃 Pumpkin 150g 305g +155g ×2.0 38.8g/h 🫐 Blueberry 75g 175g +100g ×2.3 25.0g/h

🏆 Best tesserae/hour in Juice Press: Watermelon (42.5g/h) — the fastest processing profit in the game per hour of machine time.

docs.title: Preserves Jar vs Juice Press — Which to Build?
docs.text:
Crop Preserves Price Juice Price Advantage Verdict 🥔 Potato 130g 120g Preserves +10g Jar 🥕 Carrot 120g 115g Preserves +5g Jar 🌽 Corn 165g 165g Equal Juice Press (faster) 🥬 Kale 155g 155g Equal Juice Press (faster) 🍉 Watermelon 340g 330g Preserves +10g Jar (better per unit) 🍈 Melon 300g 290g Preserves +10g Jar 🎃 Pumpkin 320g 305g Preserves +15g Jar

General rule: For high-value crops (Watermelon, Melon, Pumpkin), use the Preserves Jar for higher per-unit profit. For mids and low-value crops (Corn, Kale, Carrot), the Juice Press cycles faster, giving similar or better profit/hour.

docs.title: 🥗 Cooking Profit Analysis — Why It Matters Here
docs.text: Unlike Stardew Valley where cooking is strictly for buffs (never profit), Fields of Mistria has profitable cooking recipes. The Kitchen unlocks at House Upgrade 2, and certain dishes sell for 2-3× their ingredient cost.

docs.title: Top Profitable Dishes
docs.text:
Dish Ingredients Ingredient Cost Sell Price Profit Multiplier 🥗 Garden Salad Tomato + Lettuce 75g 200g +125g ×2.7 🍲 Vegetable Stew Potato + Carrot + Onion 98g 280g +182g ×2.9 🥟 Stuffed Cabbage Cabbage + Rice 120g 310g +190g ×2.6 🍰 Berry Tart Strawberry×2 + Wheat Flour 200g 480g +280g ×2.4 🥧 Pumpkin Pie Pumpkin + Wheat Flour + Egg 220g 550g +330g ×2.5 🧀 Cheese Plate Cheese + Grape 180g 420g +240g ×2.3 🍳 Farm Breakfast Egg + Potato + Milk 170g 400g +230g ×2.4 🥘 Hearty Stew Meat + Potato + Carrot×2 220g 520g +300g ×2.4

Key insight: Cooking can be more profitable per ingredient than artisan processing for some recipes. Vegetable Stew (2.9×) and Stuffed Cabbage (2.6×) beat most Preserves Jar routes. But cooking is limited by ingredient availability — you can't cook 200 Garden Salads if you only grow 50 tomatoes.

docs.title: When to Cook vs Process
docs.text:
Scenario Better Route You have 5-10 high-value crops Preserves Jar (passive, stacks) You have 20+ low-value crops Juice Press (fast turnover) You have specific recipe ingredients Cook (higher multiplier, instant) Endgame with kitchen fully upgraded Cook + Process (divide your harvest)

Optimal split: Process 60-70% of harvest through Preserves Jar (passive income), cook 20-30% for premium dishes, sell 10% raw for quick cash.

docs.title: 🧀 Animal Artisan Goods
docs.text:
Input Base Price Product Product Price Profit Multiplier Time 🥚 Egg 30g Mayonnaise 110g +80g ×3.7 3h 🥚 Large Egg 50g Gold Mayo 180g +130g ×3.6 3h 🥛 Milk 80g Cheese 200g +120g ×2.5 4h 🥛 Large Milk 120g Gold Cheese 280g +160g ×2.3 4h 🐑 Wool 100g Cloth 240g +140g ×2.4 4h 🐇 Rabbit Wool 150g Fine Cloth 350g +200g ×2.3 4h

Never sell raw animal products. The multiplier is too good — even a basic Egg (30g → 110g Mayo) is +267% profit. Animal processing machines should be your first artisan infrastructure after basic crop processing.

docs.title: 🌟 Perk Tree — The Hidden Profit Multiplier
docs.text: Fields of Mistria's Perk tree (unlocked via skill levels) is where the game's profit optimization gets deep. Different perk paths dramatically change your optimal strategy.

docs.title: Farming Perk Tree (Critical Nodes)
docs.text: Perk Level Effect Impact 🫙 Preservation Expert Farming 3 Preserves Jar output +15% price 🟢 High 🥤 Juice Master Farming 5 Juice Press output +15% price 🟢 High 🧑‍🍳 Gourmet Cook Farming 7 Cooked dish sell price +20% 🟢 Very High 🌿 Quality Harvest Farming 8 10% chance of double crop harvest 🟡 Medium 🏆 Master Artisan Farming 10 All artisan goods +25% price 🔴 Essential

docs.title: Optimal Perk Path
docs.text:
Early (Levels 1-4): Preservation Expert → Farming Efficiency
   ↳ Maximize Preserves Jar profit while machine count is low

Mid (Levels 5-7): Juice Master → Gourmet Cook
   ↳ Unlock Juice Press efficiency + cooking profitability

Late (Levels 8-10): Quality Harvest → Master Artisan
   ↳ Master Artisan is the single most powerful perk - +25% ALL artisan goods

⚠️ Master Artisan (+25%) is the endgame goal. It applies to Preserves Jar, Juice Press, AND animal artisan goods. Combined with Gourmet Cook (+20%), your Kitchen becomes a money printer.

docs.title: 📐 Machine Ratio Optimization
docs.text: How many of each machine should you build? The answer depends on your farm size and season.

docs.title: Early Game (30-50 crop tiles)
docs.text:
Machine Count Purpose 🫙 Preserves Jar 3-4 Primary processing 🥤 Juice Press 1-2 Fast turnover for mids 🧀 Cheese Press 1 Animal products 🫙 Mayo Machine 1 Egg processing 🍳 Kitchen 1 Cook premium dishes

Target: Process everything you harvest. Don't let crops sit.

docs.title: Mid Game (100-150 crop tiles)
docs.text:
Machine Count Purpose 🫙 Preserves Jar 8-12 Bulk processing 🥤 Juice Press 4-6 Fast turnover 🧀 Cheese Press 2-3 Animal products 🫙 Mayo Machine 3-4 Egg processing 🧥 Loom 1-2 Wool processing 🍳 Kitchen 1 Gourmet cooking

Target: 24-hour processing cycle. All daily harvests processed within the same game day.

docs.title: Endgame (250+ crop tiles, fully upgraded)
docs.text:
Machine Count Purpose 🫙 Preserves Jar 15-20 High-value crops 🥤 Juice Press 8-12 Fast mids processing 🧀 Cheese Press 4-6 Animal products 🫙 Mayo Machine 6-8 Egg processing 🧥 Loom 3-4 Wool processing 🍳 Kitchen 1 Bulk premium cooking

Target: All harvest processed. No raw crop ever touches the shipping bin.

docs.title: Plan A: Zero-Spend / Minimal Savings (Starting Spring, ~500 Tesserae)
docs.text:
Goal: Survive Spring, build 2 Preserves Jars, enter Summer with 5,000+ Tesserae.

Spring Strategy:

Week Focus Income Target 1-2 Plant Carrots (cheap, fast). Sell raw for quick cash. Build 1 Preserves Jar. 800-1,200g 3-4 Plant Cauliflower (highest spring profit/day). Process everything through the Jar. Sell raw surplus. 2,000-4,000g

Summer Entry Checkpoint: - ✅ 2+ Preserves Jars built - ✅ ~3,000-5,000g in savings - ✅ Farming Perk: Preservation Expert unlocked

Summer Strategy (Maximize Watermelon):

Action Cost Income Plant 20 Watermelon (Day 1) -1,200g — Plant 15 Corn (Day 1) -525g — Day 10: First Watermelon harvest — +3,200g raw or +6,800g preserves Corn regrows every 3 days from Day 7 — +1,050g/harvest or +2,475g juice Replant Watermelon ×20 for wave 2 -1,200g +3,200g raw

Summer Total (Zero-Spend): 15,000-25,000g net

docs.title: Plan B: Casual Player (Starting with ~5,000g savings)
docs.text:
Goal: Balanced farm with diverse income. Build all machine types.

Spring Setup:

Action Cost 15 Cauliflower (+ 15 Strawberry) seeds 750g + 900g 3 Preserves Jars 450g materials 1 Juice Press (Town Rank 2) 300g materials Chicken + Coop 1,500g

Mid-Summer (After first big harvests):

Income Source Weekly Tesserae Watermelon Preserves (20 tiles) ~6,800g/week Corn Juice (20 tiles) ~3,300g/week Mayo (3 chickens) ~1,540g/week Cooking (Vegetable Stew) ~2,000g/week Total ~13,640g/week

docs.title: Plan C: Speedrun / Optimizer (Starting with 20,000g+ savings or massive Spring)
docs.text:
Goal: Full automation + maximum g/day by Fall Year 1.

Summer Day 1 Mass Planting:

Crop Tiles Seed Cost Strategy 🍉 Watermelon 60 3,600g Preserves Jar (best per-unit) 🌽 Corn 40 1,400g Juice Press (best per-hour) 🍈 Melon 30 1,650g Preserves Jar (high value) 🫐 Blueberry 30 1,200g Juice Press or Jam 🍅 Tomato 20 600g Preserves or Cook (Salad)

Infrastructure:

Machine Count Total Cost 🫙 Preserves Jar 12 ~1,800g 🥤 Juice Press 8 ~1,200g 🍳 Kitchen Upgrade 1 5,000g Mayo Machine 4 ~600g 🧀 Cheese Press 2 ~400g Coop + Barn 1 each 4,000g

Daily Income (Summer Peak):

Source Daily Watermelon Preserves (60 tiles, wave cycles) 6,800g/day avg Corn Juice (40 tiles, every 3 days) 2,200g/day avg Melon Preserves (30 tiles) 2,400g/day avg Blueberry Juice (30 tiles) 1,750g/day avg Animal Products (processed) 1,200g/day Cooking (Garden Salad + Vegetable Stew) 2,500g/day Total Daily Income ~16,850g/day Season Total ~470,000-500,000g

docs.title: 🏆 Endgame Maximum Profit Configuration
docs.text: Once you have Master Artisan (+25%) and Gourmet Cook (+20%), this is the optimal endgame farm:

docs.title: The Ultimate Preserves + Cooking Split
docs.text: Activity % of Harvest Machine Count Notes Preserves Jar 50% 20 Best for Watermelon, Pumpkin, Melon Juice Press 20% 10 Best for Corn, Blueberry Cooking 20% Kitchen Vegetable Stew, Berry Tart, Pumpkin Pie Animal Artisan 10% 12 total Mayo + Cheese + Loom

docs.title: Estimated Endgame Weekly Income (with Master Artisan + Gourmet Cook)
docs.text:
Season Weekly Income Strategy 🌸 Spring 50,000-70,000g Cauliflower Preserves + Strawberry Tart ☀️ Summer 100,000-150,000g Watermelon Preserves + Corn Juice + Vegetable Stew 🍂 Fall 80,000-120,000g Pumpkin Pie + Cranberry Jam + Artichoke Preserves ❄️ Winter 15,000-25,000g Animal products + stored cooked goods

Winter Tip: Stockpile cooked dishes in Fall. Pumpkin Pies and Vegetable Stews have long shelf life. Sell them in Winter when there's no fresh crop income.

docs.title: Year 1 Target Benchmarks
docs.text: Milestone Target Check End of Spring 5,000-10,000g Preserves Jar + basic animal setup End of Summer 50,000-100,000g Full machine setup + chickens/cows End of Fall 150,000-250,000g Master Artisan + Kitchen fully upgraded Year 1 Total 200,000-350,000g All upgrades + fully automated farm

docs.title: 🍳 Fields of Mistria — Cooking Deep Guide & Recipe Compendium
docs.text: Cooking is your most important energy management tool and stat-boost source in the mid-to-late game. Once your kitchen is upgraded, you can turn crops into high-value meals with far better energy/gold efficiency than selling raw ingredients.

docs.title: 🏠 Kitchen Upgrades & Unlocks
docs.text:
Tier Unlock Condition Cost Unlocks Basic Kitchen House Upgrade Lv.2 (Cabin → Farmhouse) 3000g + 50 Wood Basic stove, 2 cooking slots Mid Kitchen Town Rank B 8000g + 80 Stone + 5 Iron Bars Oven, fermenting vat, 4 slots Advanced Kitchen Town Rank A 18000g + 10 Gold Bars + 3 Mystery Gems Magic Stove (+15% double-produce chance)

Tip: The Mid Kitchen is the best value upgrade — the fermenting vat lets you batch-produce energy meals and effectively doubles your mining recovery.

docs.title: Energy Recovery Meals
docs.text: Dish ⭐ Rarity Ingredients Energy HP Cost Sell Price Profit 🥗 Garden Salad Common Lettuce×1 + Tomato×1 45 30 20g 110g 90g 🍲 Veggie Soup Common Carrot×2 + Potato×1 60 40 35g 160g 125g 🥟 Mushroom Pie Rare Mushroom×2 + Flour×1 + Egg×1 90 60 55g 250g 195g 🍝 Cream Pasta Rare Wheat×2 + Milk×1 + Cheese×1 110 80 70g 320g 250g 🥩 Grilled Platter Epic Cooked Meat×1 + Potato×2 + Onion×1 160 120 120g 480g 360g 🍰 Harvest Cake Legendary Pumpkin×1 + Egg×2 + Honey×1 + Cream×1 220 180 180g 750g 570g

docs.title: Stat Boost Meals
docs.text:
Dish Effect Duration Ingredients Energy Restored 🌶️ Spicy Stew +3 Mining, +2 Attack 5min Chili×2 + Meat×1 + Tomato×1 80 🐟 Grilled Fish Set +4 Fishing, +1 Speed 5min Fish×2 + Lemon×1 + Butter×1 70 🧊 Icy Fruit Drink +2 Energy/sec regen 3min Blueberry×3 + Honey×1 + Ice×1 50 🍄 Magic Broth +20% Magic Damage 4min Magic Mushroom×1 + Milk×2 + Sugar×1 100 🥊 Warrior's Meal +5 Attack, +3 Defense 6min Meat×3 + Egg×2 + Chili×1 130 🦐 Seafood Feast +3 Luck, +2 Fishing 6min Shrimp×2 + Clam×3 + Lemon×1 + Butter×1 150

Tip: Buff durations don't stack, but eating a new dish refreshes the timer. Eat Spicy Stew before heading into the mines, then chug a Warrior's Meal before combat — the two buffs coexist fine.

docs.title: 🎯 Best Dish by Scenario
docs.text: Scenario 🥇 Best Dish Why ⛏️ Deep Mining Mushroom Pie 90 energy / 55g cost = 1.64 energy/g ratio — you'll need 8–10 for 20 floors 🎣 Fishing Marathon Grilled Fish Set +4 Fishing upgrades a Copper Rod to equivalent of Iron; lets you catch legendaries ⚔️ Boss Fight Warrior's Meal + Magic Broth 6 minutes of dual buffs covers the entire Golem Guardian fight 💰 Pure Profit Harvest Cake 180g cost → 750g sale = 570g net, 120% more than selling the pumpkin raw 🌾 Daily Farm Work Garden Salad 45 energy for only 20g — the best energy/g ratio on the table (2.25) 🧪 Skill Grinding Cream Pasta Highest XP — 110 energy + 250g profit, leveling skills while earning gold

docs.title: 📊 Energy-to-Profit Conversion Chain
docs.text:
Route Ingredients Steps Total Profit Energy/g Rating Sell Pumpkin Raw Pumpkin×1 None 150g 0.50 ⭐ Pumpkin → Harvest Cake Pumpkin + Egg×2 + Honey + Cream Kitchen +420g 1.22 ⭐⭐⭐ Wheat → Flour → Cream Pasta Wheat×2 + Milk + Cheese Mill → Cook +250g 1.57 ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Tomato + Lettuce → Salad Tomato + Lettuce Direct cook +90g 2.25 ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐

Profit Formula: Net profit per dish = Sell price − Ingredient opportunity cost. Salad may look low-value per dish, but its ingredients cost only 20g for 45 energy — it's the king of energy conversion efficiency.

docs.title: 🌸 Seasonal Ingredient Stockpile Plan
docs.text:
Season Must-Hoard Ingredients Use Stockpile Target 🌸 Spring Lettuce, Tomato, Carrot, Potato Salad/Soup (year-round mining rations) 50+ each ☀️ Summer Chili, Blueberry, Corn, Wheat Spicy Stew / Icy Drink / Flour base Chili 30+, Wheat 60+ 🍂 Fall Pumpkin, Onion, Mushroom Harvest Cake / Cream Pasta (winter staple) Pumpkin 40+, Mushroom 30+ ❄️ Winter Stockpile consumption + Greenhouse Use fall stock for high-profit dishes No new harvest — live off seasonal reserves

Winter Strategy: Stockpile 40 pumpkins + 30 mushrooms in fall. Produce Harvest Cakes (+570g each) and Cream Pasta (+250g each) daily in winter. Pair with greenhouse strawberries (4-day grow cycle) for 2000g+ daily cooking profit.

docs.title: 🧮 Cooking Skill Specialization Path
docs.text:
Specialization Effect Unlock Condition Priority Ingredient Master All dishes restore +15% energy Cook 10 dishes 🥇 First Double Cooking 15% chance to make two at once Cook 30 dishes 🥇 Gourmet Buff duration +50% Cook 50 dishes 🥈 Profit Nose Dish sell price +20% Town Rank A 🥈 Magic Cooking Can make magic dishes (special effects) Complete wizard tower quest 🥉

Tip: Unlock Ingredient Master first — 15% more energy from every dish means 2 fewer meals in the mines = 6 extra backpack slots.

Data source: Fields of Mistria v0.8+ (Early Access), playtested data

docs.title: ⚒️ Fields of Mistria — Crafting & Forging Guide
docs.text: Crafting is the core engine of wealth in Mistria. A single processing machine can boost crop value by 150%–250%, but your equipment order and recipe choices determine how fast your cash flows.

docs.title: 🛠️ Workbench Unlock Order
docs.text:
# Device Unlock Condition Materials Function 1 Basic Workbench Starting 10 Wood + 5 Stone Basic tools / fences 2 🔥 Furnace Quest: Fix the Mines 20 Stone + 10 Copper Ore Ore → Metal Bars 3 🔨 Anvil Obtain 2 Iron Bars 30 Stone + 5 Iron Bars Weapon / tool upgrades 4 🧵 Loom Obtain Wool 40 Wood + 10 Iron Bars Wool → Cloth (160g) 5 🪡 Spinning Wheel Loom+ 30 Wood + 5 Copper Bars Cloth → Silk (320g) 6 🫙 Pickle Jar Town Rank D 50 Stone + 10 Copper Bars + 5 Clay Veggies → Pickles (1.8×) 7 🍷 Keg Town Rank C 60 Wood + 15 Copper + 10 Iron Crops → Wine (2.5×) 8 🍯 Beehive Town Rank C 40 Wood + 5 Iron + Flower Flower → Honey (200g+) 9 ✨ Magic Workshop Complete wizard tower quest 8 Gold Bars + 5 Mystery Gems Enchant / magic gear

Tip: Furnace is priority one — no metal bars means no tool upgrades and no follow-up machines. Your week 1 goal is gathering 20 Stone for the furnace.

docs.title: Wine vs. Pickles vs. Jam
docs.text:
Ingredient Sell Price 🫙 Pickles (1.8×) Time Daily Profit 🍷 Wine (2.5×) Time Daily Profit Pumpkin 150g 270g 2d 60g 375g 4d 56g Strawberry 85g 153g 1d 68g 212g 3d 42g Watermelon 160g 288g 2d 64g 400g 4d 60g Tomato 60g 108g 1d 48g 150g 2d 45g

Bottom line: Pickles win on turnover, Wine wins on per-batch margin. Early game when you're short on machines, prioritize Pickle Jars (1-day turnaround). Switch to Kegs later when your stockpile is overflowing.

docs.title: Honey & Silk
docs.text:
Machine Input Output Sell Price Processing Time Daily Profit Beehive Flowers (placed Spring/Summer/Fall) Seasonal honey 200–350g 3d 67–117g Spinning Wheel Cloth×1 Silk 320g 1d 160g Loom Wool×2 Cloth 160g 1d 80g

Honey Strategy: Spring blue flower honey 200g → Summer sunflower honey 280g → Fall magic flower honey 350g. Place beehives near your main planting zones and harvest every 3 days. Start with 4 beehives for 400g+ daily profit (not counting labor).

docs.title: 🧪 Optimal Crafting Route Decision Tree
docs.text:
Crops
├── High-value single-harvest (Watermelon / Pumpkin)
│   └── 🍷 Wine (long maturing, wait 4 days for high price)
├── Low-value fast crops (Tomato / Carrot)
│   └── 🫙 Pickles (1-day fast turnover, keeps cash flowing)
├── Regrow crops (Strawberry / Blueberry / Corn)
│   ├── 🫙 Pickles (single harvest → 1-day ROI → reinvest)
│   └── 🍯 Or keep fresh (Corn → chicken feed)
└── Flowers
    └── 🍯 Beehive (3-day honey, zero labor cost)

Capital Efficiency Formula: Daily Profit = (Processing Price − Ingredient Opportunity Cost) ÷ Processing Days. Tight on cash early on? Go pickles (high daily profit). Late-game loaded? Go wine (higher total profit).

docs.title: ✨ Enchantment System: Magic Tool Upgrades
docs.text:
Once the Magic Workshop is unlocked, use Mystery Gems to enchant your tools and weapons.

Tool Enchant Effect Materials Needed Rating ⛏️ Hoe Range+1 / Energy cost −25% 3 Mystery Gems 🥇 🪓 Axe Hardwood drop rate +50% / Speed+1 3 Mystery Gems 🥇 💧 Watering Can Range+1 / Fan-shaped watering area 3 Mystery Gems 🥇 ⚔️ Sword Lifesteal 3% / Crit rate +10% / Elemental damage 5 Mystery Gems 🥈 🪚 Scythe Harvest range+1 / Hay drop rate +50% 3 Mystery Gems 🥈 ⛏️ Pickaxe Mining speed+2 / Double ore chance +15% 3 Mystery Gems 🥉

Enchant Order: Hoe > Watering Can > Axe > Sword > Scythe > Pickaxe. Range+1 on Hoe and Watering Can cuts daily farm work by 40% — the best time management investment you can make.

Tip: Mystery Gems mainly drop from gem veins on mine floors 11–20, Boss drops, and fishing treasure chests. Use your first 10 for enchanting tools; sell any extras for 800g each.

docs.title: 🗺️ Rare Material Farming Guide
docs.text: Material Use How to Get Rarity Mystery Gem Enchanting Floors 11–20 (15%) / Chests (5%) ⭐⭐⭐ Magic Mushroom Cooking / Enchanting Floors 15–20 (daily respawn) ⭐⭐⭐ Ancient Seed Greenhouse special crop Ancient ruins dig (3%) / Chests (2%) ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Dragon Scale Ore Legendary weapon Floor 20 Boss (25%) ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Silk Thread Silk / premium cloth Spinning Wheel (Cloth → Silk) ⭐⭐ Hardwood Advanced buildings Mystic Forest (daily respawn, Axe Lv.3+) ⭐⭐ Clay Pickle Jar / buildings Hoe ground (15%) / Mine floors 1–5 ⭐

docs.title: 🌳 Crafting Skill Tree
docs.text:
Skill Level Effect Priority Efficient Processing Lv.3 Machine time −15% 🥇 Double Output Lv.5 10% chance for double yield 🥇 Quality Boost Lv.7 Processed goods quality +1 tier 🥈 Batch Production Lv.9 Can input 2× materials at once 🥈 Magic Crafter Lv.10 25% chance for magic version (+50% sell price) 🥉 Enchant Master Lv.12 Enchant costs −1 gem 🥉

Leveling Path: Lv.3 Efficient Processing → Lv.5 Double Output → Lv.7 Quality Boost → Lv.10 Magic Crafter. Process 200+ items to push your level.

Tip: Don't neglect beehives — they give free XP regardless of your crafting level. 3 days per honey batch = 30–50 free XP every 3 days. Run 4 long-term for 2400g/month + 600+ XP.

Data source: Fields of Mistria v0.8+ (Early Access)

docs.title: Fields of Mistria — Crop Profit Guide
docs.text:
Maximize your farm's profitability in Fields of Mistria with this comprehensive crop profit guide. The table below covers every spring, summer, and fall crop including seed price, growth time, sell price, and profit per day.

Note: Prices shown are base values. Crop quality, ranching perks, and festival bonuses can increase sell prices significantly.

docs.title: Quick Tips
docs.text:
• Early game: Plant Carrots (Spring) and Onions (Summer) — cheap and fast-growing for quick cash.
  • Mid game: Invest in Strawberries (Spring) and Cranberries (Fall) — regrowth crops give sustained income.
  • Late game: Watermelons (Summer) and Pumpkins (Fall) have the highest single-harvest profit.
  • Processing: Always run high-volume crops like Tomato, Corn, and Potato through the Preserves Jar for maximum value.
  • Seed Maker: Use on expensive regrowth crops (Strawberry, Cranberry) to save money on replanting.

Data source: Fields of Mistria Wiki 1.0
